Microsoft teams can [learn more about this effort and share feedback](https://docs.opensource.microsoft.com/releasing/maintain/templates/) within the open source guidance available internally.

title: [Feature Request]: Add an option to match system theme automatically (On terminal Windows, not only decorations
author: RoPedro
body:
### Description of the new feature

Terminal can correctly match Windows theme on Decorations and menus, but not on the terminal panels itself, I propose an option that matches a profile color scheme with the Terminal (or Windows theme)

### Proposed technical implementation details

on `Profiles > <my_profile> > Appearance > Color Scheme`, a theme with the name "Match Terminal" or "Match System"

Then on Sunrise, the theme would be something similar to maybe `Tango Light`, and on Sunset, something like `Campbell/Vintage`

title: Some Unicode characters are broken

body: ### Windows Terminal version 1.24.11321.0 ### Windows build number 10.0.26310.0 ### Other Software PowerShell 7.6.3 Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS (also reproduces) ### Steps to reproduce 1) Open WT with e.g. pwsh.exe profile, write: `क्त्र` or `स्त्र्य ` ### Expected Behavior 1) the specific grapheme cluster(s) are written occupying expected number of cells and occupying only cells where it's visible ### Actual Behavior Grapheme clusters are written with a trailing seemingly whitespace cells clearly containing data (deleting changes the cluster). --- I'd also check: - Indic conjunct - Myanmar marks

title: Crash (0xc0000005 in Microsoft.Terminal.Control.dll) when drag-merging a tab into another window on 1.24, all windows lost
author: danielw98
body:
### Windows Terminal version

1.24.11321.0 (package Microsoft.WindowsTerminal_1.24.11321.0_x64__8wekyb3d8bbwe, app version 1.24.2605.12001)

### Windows build number

10.0.26200.8655 (Windows 11 Enterprise 25H2)

### Other Software

Each tab runs pwsh 7 hosting the Claude Code CLI (Node.js based), which streams large volumes of output, so scrollback buffers were large at crash time. Roughly 8 tabs/windows total.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open several Terminal windows (about 8), each running a long-lived process producing heavy streaming output, and let scrollback grow for an hour or more.
2. Drag a tab from one window and drop it onto another window to merge it.
3. Intermittently, WindowsTerminal.exe crashes with an access violation. Because all windows share the one WindowsTerminal.exe process, every terminal on the desktop dies at once.

Reproduces intermittently but has hit multiple times on this machine, always during the drag-merge gesture, never at rest.

### Expected Behavior

The tab moves to the target window. A failure in the transfer should at worst affect that tab, not take down every terminal window.

### Actual Behavior

WindowsTerminal.exe dies with 0xc0000005 and all windows close. Application Event Log at the crash:

```
Faulting application name: WindowsTerminal.exe, version: 1.24.2605.12001, time stamp: 0x6a03a6ca
Faulting module name: Microsoft.Terminal.Control.dll, version: 1.24.2605.12001, time stamp: 0x6a03a3a2
Exception code: 0xc0000005
Fault offset: 0x000000000002c924
Faulting process id: 0x2680
Report Id: a91e486b-89d0-4c75-b908-1db40506b70f
WER fault bucket: 1529479043225831888, type 5, Event Name: MoAppCrash
```

Possibly relevant: 30 minutes before the crash, WER logged RADAR_PRE_LEAK_64 against the same WindowsTerminal.exe process (memory growth flagged), consistent with the large scrollback load.

### Additional context

This looks like the same class as #17246 (crash when drag-merging a tab between windows, faulting in Microsoft.Terminal.Control.dll), which was milestoned for v1.24 and closed. It still reproduces on 1.24.2605.12001 with the signature above.

A local crash dump was captured (WindowsTerminal.exe PID 0x2680, 46 MB) and is available on request. It contains terminal scrollback content, so I would prefer to share it through a private channel rather than attach it here.

title: RUNS

title: Terminal keeps crashing without a message/trace (when device is locked)
author: msarris
body:
### Windows Terminal version

1.24.11321.0

### Windows build number

26200.7171

### Other Software

_No response_

### Steps to reproduce

I'd say this is going to be pretty hard to reproduce, but it happens like this:
- I have Terminal open in a WSL session (Ubuntu).
- I lock my laptop ([Windows] + [L]), laptop stays running and doesn't hibernate or anything.
- When I get back and unlock Terminal is gone, without any message or error, just seems to have quit silently.

But this only happens sometimes. Sometimes not for a day, then other days multiple times per day (like today).

### Expected Behavior

Terminal stays open and running when my device is locked.

### Actual Behavior

Terminal is suddenly gone when I get back.

I've never experienced it closing with me actually behind the laptop, only when it's locked. Even after 10 minutes after locking it can suddenly be gone.

title: Opt-in automation surface (list sessions, read buffers, send input) so agent/MCP tooling can drive the terminal
author: steven-pribilinskiy
body:
## Description of the new feature

AI coding agents live in terminal panes now, and the tooling around them keeps needing the same three primitives from the terminal that hosts them: enumerate sessions/panes, read a pane's buffer, and send input to a specific pane. Every other major terminal ships some opt-in form of this today. Windows Terminal ships none of it, so it's become the one terminal where this whole class of tooling dead-ends.

Concrete case from my own tooling. I maintain a small CLI that bookmarks and renames AI coding sessions (Claude Code). A live rename means injecting a `/rename <title>` command into the exact pane hosting a given session, identified by the session id its statusline renders into the buffer. The flow today:

- **tmux**: `list-panes` + `capture-pane` to find the pane whose buffer shows the session id, then `send-keys`
- **[Tabby](https://github.com/Eugeny/tabby)**: the identical flow through its community MCP plugin, [tabby-mcp-server](https://github.com/thuanpham582002/tabby-mcp-server): `get_session_list`, `get_terminal_buffer`, `send_input`
- **kitty**: the [remote control protocol](https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/remote-control/) (`kitty @ ls` / `get-text` / `send-text`), opt-in via `allow_remote_control` with an optional password
- **iTerm2**: the [Python API](https://iterm2.com/python-api/), off by default with explicit user consent
- **WezTerm**: [`wezterm cli send-text`](https://wezfurlong.org/wezterm/cli/cli/send-text.html) and `wezterm cli list`
- **Windows Terminal**: nothing. The tool prints "paste `/rename <title>` yourself"

There's nothing to build on here: `wt.exe` subcommands are structural only (`new-tab`, `split-pane`, `focus-tab`, `move-focus`, `move-pane`, `swap-pane`, `focus-pane`), there's no enumeration that carries content, no buffer read, no input path. The only read surface is UIA, which is an accessibility contract, not an automation one.

The MCP angle is what makes the gap so visible in 2026. Community MCP servers already exist for iTerm2 ([ferrislucas/iterm-mcp](https://github.com/ferrislucas/iterm-mcp)), tmux ([nickgnd/tmux-mcp](https://github.com/nickgnd/tmux-mcp)) and Tabby (linked above), so any MCP-capable agent can drive those terminals out of the box. Nobody can build the equivalent for Windows Terminal because there's no control surface underneath to wrap.

I know `wt send-input` was rejected a few months ago (#9368, PR #20106) over the injection risk, and that rejection makes sense for what it was: an unauthenticated CLI verb any process on the box could invoke. This proposal is a different shape. It's the opt-in, authenticated, consent-gated model that kitty and iTerm2 already ship. And half the value is the read side (session enumeration + buffer), which #9368 never covered and which carries a much smaller risk profile than injection.

## Proposed technical implementation details

- Off by default. No endpoint exists unless the user turns it on
- Consent can't live in settings.json alone. @zadjii-msft's concern in #9368 was that malicious.exe could just edit the settings file, agreed. So: a UI-only toggle persisted outside settings.json, plus a first-connection consent prompt that names the connecting process (the iTerm2 per-script consent model, it works)
- Transport: an ACL'd named pipe (caller identity comes for free) or localhost HTTP with a per-boot token
- Hard elevation boundary: elevated windows and tabs are never serviceable, not even by opt-in. That keeps the mixed-elevation concern that killed #9368 out of scope entirely
- Minimal v1 surface: `list_sessions` (window/tab/pane tree with ids and titles), `read_buffer(pane, lastNLines)`, `send_input(pane, text)`. Input could sit behind a second, separate opt-in so read-only automation never carries injection risk
- A visible indicator while an automation client is connected (tab decoration or statusline badge), so it's never silent
- MCP itself doesn't need to be first-party. Ship the control surface and the community will build the MCP server on top, exactly the way it happened for iTerm2, tmux and Tabby

title: Settings UI: dismissing a ComboBox dropdown by clicking another control also activates that control (light-dismiss click passes through)
author: steven-pribilinskiy
body:
## Description

In the Settings UI, when a ComboBox dropdown is open and the user clicks outside it to dismiss, the click is **not** consumed by the light-dismiss — it also lands on whatever control is underneath. Example from the profile Appearance page: with the color scheme dropdown open, clicking the expander's chevron both closes the dropdown **and** toggles the expander.

`Flyout`-based popovers behave correctly by comparison: the "More colors…" flyout on the same page consumes the outside click when light-dismissing.

This affects every ComboBox in the Settings UI (it likely stems from light-dismiss handling in the XAML island hosting the settings). `LightDismissOverlayMode="On"` per-ComboBox would consume the click, but dims the window and would make individual combos inconsistent with the rest of the app, so it doesn't look like the right fix.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open Settings → any profile → Appearance.
2. Open the "Color scheme" ComboBox dropdown.
3. Click the expander chevron (or any other control) outside the open dropdown.

**Expected:** the dropdown closes; the click is consumed (matching Flyout light-dismiss behavior).
**Actual:** the dropdown closes and the underlying control activates (the expander toggles).

Observed on Windows Terminal stable and on a local `main` build. Split out of PR #20390 validation; not introduced by that PR.

title: Settings UI: expander header hover highlight fires while hovering interactive controls inside the header (e.g. color scheme ComboBox)
author: steven-pribilinskiy
body:
## Description

In the Settings UI, some `SettingContainer` expanders host interactive controls in their header's "current value" slot — e.g. the color scheme ComboBox on the profile Appearance page (both in released builds and in the reworked pair UI of #20390).

Because the WinUI 2 `Expander` header is a ToggleButton whose PointerOver visual state reacts to the pointer anywhere over the header subtree, hovering the ComboBox lights up the entire header row as if the expand/collapse toggle were about to be clicked. That reads as misleading affordance: the user is targeting the combo, not the toggle.

The repo does not override the Expander ControlTemplate (only theme brushes like `ExpanderHeaderBorderBrush` in `SettingContainerStyle.xaml`), and the header toggle's hover-state resources are internal to WinUI's compiled template, so there is no lightweight scoped-resource fix — suppressing or scoping the hover visual appears to require retemplating the Expander header (or moving interactive content out of headers).

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open Settings → any profile → Appearance.
2. Hover the pointer over the "Color scheme" ComboBox in the collapsed expander header.

**Expected:** only the ComboBox shows hover feedback.
**Actual:** the whole expander header shows its hover highlight as well.

Observed on Windows Terminal stable and on a local `main` build. Split out of PR #20390 validation; not introduced by that PR.

title: Support per-theme (dark/light) foreground, background, selectionBackground and cursorColor overrides
author: steven-pribilinskiy
body:
## Description of the new feature

A profile appearance can already use a different color scheme per app theme:

```json
"colorScheme": { "dark": "Campbell", "light": "One Half Light" }
```

However, the explicit appearance-level color overrides — `foreground`, `background`, `selectionBackground`, `cursorColor` — are single, theme-independent values (`AppearanceConfig` stores one nullable color each). At runtime the active theme first resolves the dark or light scheme, and then the same override is layered on top regardless of which scheme was chosen (`TerminalSettings::_ApplyAppearanceSettings`).

When a scheme pair is in use, this can produce broken results. Example: `colorScheme: { dark: "Campbell", light: "One Half Light" }` plus `background: "#282C34"` renders the light theme with One Half Light's dark-on-light foreground colors over a dark gray background — effectively unreadable, with no way to express "only override the background in dark mode".

**Proposal:** allow the explicit overrides to accept the same dark/light object form as `colorScheme`:

```json
"foreground": { "dark": "#DCDFE4", "light": "#383A42" },
"background": { "dark": "#282C34", "light": "#FAFAFA" }
```

with the existing scalar form remaining valid (applies to both themes, current behavior, fully backwards compatible).

## Proposed technical implementation details

- `AppearanceConfig`: split the four nullable overrides into dark/light variants with the same shared-when-scalar serialization pattern used by `DarkColorSchemeName`/`LightColorSchemeName` (serialize the pair form only when the two values differ).
- `TerminalSettings::_ApplyAppearanceSettings`: pick the override variant matching the resolved theme, after scheme resolution.
- Settings UI: the Appearance page's Foreground/Background/Selection background/Cursor color editors would follow whichever presentation lands for the scheme pair UI (see #16984 / PR #20390); the preview swatches currently deduce their "no override" default from the dark scheme only, which this work could also address.

## Context

Split out of PR #20390 (Settings UI for choosing separate light & dark color schemes, implementing #16984), where this interaction was noticed during validation. Keeping that PR scoped to the scheme pair UI; this issue tracks making the explicit overrides theme-aware as follow-up.

title: Support binding ColorSchema to Windows theme
author: vasekboch
body:
### Description of the new feature

Would be awesome to be able setup for each profile separate ColorScheme for LightMode and DarkMode. So when you switch to LightMode in Windows you have Light Schema in Terminal as well and vice versa. 

I use DarkMode, but when I'm ouside, for better readability I enable LightMode in Windows. But I end up with Dark terminals. And I need to switch each tab manually. Yes I could script it but seems like a good feature to be build in. 

### Proposed technical implementation details

Add
```
"colorSchemeLight": "One Half Light",
"colorSchemeDark": "One Half Dark",
```
And follow the Dark/Light option that results from `theme` settings.

title: Dropdown menus scroll out of Terminal and screen in settings
author: justinnas
body:
### Windows Terminal version

1.24.11321.0

### Windows build number

10.0.26200.8655

### Other Software

_No response_

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open Terminal (not in full screen).
2. Open settings (tab section -> "v" menu -> "Settings").
3. Click and open any dropdown menu (e.g. "Startup": "Default profile").
4. Scroll down in the Terminal.
5. See that the dropdown menu scrolls away out of the Terminal.

### Expected Behavior

The expected behavior would be to lock scroll of the Terminal while a dropdown menu is visible (at least that's how it's done while tab "v" menu is open and overall in Windows Settings).

### Actual Behavior

The dropdown scrolls together with the Terminal UI and goes out of the Terminal and at times even out of the screen.

title: Multi-second freeze of the whole window when switching input language (Win+Space / Alt+Shift) with many tabs open
author: Profit155
body:
## Windows Terminal version

1.24.11321.0

## Windows build number

10.0.26100 (Windows 11 IoT Enterprise LTSC 2024, 24H2)

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open Windows Terminal and accumulate a large number of tabs (~60+ tabs; in my case 37 live OpenConsole/ConPTY hosts and ~66 shell processes, all hosted by a single WindowsTerminal process).
2. Focus any tab (a plain PowerShell prompt is enough — no TUI app required).
3. Switch the Windows input language (e.g. between English and Russian) using either Alt+Shift or Win+Space.
4. Try to keep typing.

## Expected Behavior

The input language switches instantly, as it does in other applications (Notepad switches with no perceptible delay on the same machine at the same time).

## Actual Behavior

The whole Windows Terminal window freezes for roughly 2–3 seconds on every input language switch: no keyboard input is processed and rendering stalls. After the freeze, everything resumes normally.

Additional observations:

- Reproduces in any tab, with any shell (plain PowerShell prompt, or a TUI app like Claude Code — makes no difference).
- Both Alt+Shift and Win+Space trigger the identical freeze, so it is not hotkey-specific.
- A plain focus change (Alt+Tab away and back) does NOT cause any freeze — only the input language change does.
- Other applications on the same machine (e.g. Notepad) switch input language instantly, so it does not look like a system-wide TSF/ctfmon problem. `ctfmon.exe` and `TextInputHost.exe` are running and responsive; the `TextInputManagementService` service is Running/Automatic.
- The machine has 2 input layouts installed (en-US 0409:00000409, ru 0419:00000419), `IsLegacyLanguageBar=False`, `IsLegacySwitchingMode=False`.
- Subjectively the delay has grown over time as my typical tab count grew (it was not noticeable in the past with fewer tabs), which makes me suspect the language-change handling does per-tab/per-control work (TSF input context updates?) serially on the UI thread. I have not yet done a controlled bisection of freeze duration vs. tab count, so please treat the scaling hypothesis as unverified — but the freeze itself with ~60 tabs is 100% reproducible.

Happy to collect ETW traces / WPR captures or run a controlled tab-count bisection if that helps.

title: Add setting for optional confirmation prompt before closing individual tabs
author: fn5
body:
### Description of the new feature

I am writing to request that Windows Terminal to provide an option to confirm before closing an individual tab.

At the moment, it is relatively easy to accidentally close a tab, particularly when working with multiple tabs or when trying to select a tab near the close button. This can be disruptive when the tab contains an active SSH session, long-running process, elevated shell, development environment, or other state that cannot easily be restored.

Windows Terminal already provides protection in some close scenarios, such as warning before closing multiple tabs. However, there does not appear to be an equivalent option for closing a single tab.

This feature would allow users to opt in to a confirmation prompt before an individual tab is closed.
I have a personal reason in that sometimes my index finger is a little trigger happy, even twitchy after a long day of work, and the loss of a tab and its scrollback, can be a real shame.

I believe this was raised earlier (https://github.com/microsoft/terminal/issues/9470) but it was closed without an implementation.

### Proposed technical implementation details

Proposed behaviour

Add a setting that allows users to enable confirmation before closing a tab.
For example, when enabled, closing a tab could show a prompt such as:

> Close this tab?  
> This will terminate the running shell or process in this tab.

With options such as:
- **Close tab**
- **Cancel**

Ideally, this could be configurable at one or more of the following levels:
- Globally for all tabs
- Per profile

### Possible settings design
A global setting might look like:

`"confirmCloseTab": true`

A per-profile setting might look like:

```
"profiles": {
    "list": [
        {
            "name": "SSH",
            "confirmCloseTab": true
        }
    ]
}
```

Possible values for the setting could include:
```
"confirmCloseTab": "never"
"confirmCloseTab": "always"
"confirmCloseTab": "whenProcessRunning"
```

title: Visually unify the "Default Profile" and "Default Terminal" drop-downs
author: pronichkin
body: ### Description of the new feature These two drop-downs follow the same pattern _conceptually,_ but not _visually._ They **should** look similar. - Should have the same width. - Should have the same style. More specifically, the Default Profile should display the vendor and app version. - At very least, that should work for auto-generated profiled for well-known apps where we know for sure how to query those properties. - Bonus points if we could allow users to specify arbitrary provider name and version for the custom profiles they create manually. ### Proposed technical implementation details _No response_

title: Wrap to next tab when attempting to move to pane beyond edge of current tab
author: TetraK1
body:
### Description of the new feature

In Zellij, when you are in the rightmost pane of a tab, and you use the keybind for moving to the next pane to the right, you instead move to the leftmost pane in the next tab.  When in the leftmost pane of a tab, moving a pane to the left sends you to the rightmost pane of the previous tab.  I would like to see this in terminal.

### Proposed technical implementation details

I have basic behaviour (i.e. the easiest bit) for this working, but not tests or settings.

There are still questions about which pane to switch to if you have multiple panes at the edge of the new tab, that would be viable candidates e.g. do you
- try to pick the pane that is always the topmost one in the new tab
- try to align verticality of the new pane?
- go to the last focused pane in the new tab?

The logic for moving panes generally could probably apply here, and treat the join between tabs as just another pane separator (join the pane trees of the two tabs, as if they were a horizontal split at the highest level)

title: Feature Request: Add a native 'matrix' command for text rendering benchmarks and classic easter egg
author: asanalinskiy
body:
### Description of the new feature

### Description
I would like to propose adding a native, built-in command called `matrix` to the Windows Terminal / Console environment. This command would launch a high-performance, native Matrix Digital Rain animation directly in the terminal buffer.

### Theoretical & Practical Value
1. **Rendering Benchmark:** The classic Matrix rain is an excellent real-world stress test for text rendering pipelines, scrollback buffers, and ANSI escape sequence processing speed under heavy load. It updates the entire grid rapidly, making it perfect for benchmarking frame drops and CPU overhead in the terminal.
2. **Developer Culture:** Famous command-line tools and operating systems have always embraced iconic terminal culture features (such as `sl` in Linux or `screenSaver` utilities). Adding a native `matrix` command out-of-the-box would be a highly celebrated addition for developers, sysadmins, and power users worldwide.

### Technical Implementation Details
To ensure it adheres to Windows performance standards, the tool should be written in pure, native C++ using standard APIs and modern virtual terminal sequences:
- **Zero Dependencies:** Compiles directly via MSVC/GCC into a tiny binary with no runtime overhead.
- **Flicker-Free Design:** Collects the entire character grid layout into a single string memory buffer before flushing to `stdout` via `\033[H`, preventing screen tearing.
- **Localization Ready:** Uses `CP_UTF8` to correctly output authentic Japanese Katakana Unicode ranges (`0x30A1` - `0x30F6`) natively.

### Reference Implementation
I have developed a fully functional, production-ready C++ core implementation that achieves rock-solid 60 FPS performance with minimal CPU usage. You can review the repository and source code structure here:
[here source code](https://github.com/asanaliiiiii/matrix)

Thank you for considering this proposal to bring a piece of classic terminal history natively into modern Windows!

### Proposed technical implementation details

_No response_
